The application is a fullstack web app built with React 18 and TypeScript on the frontend, Python Django 4.2 with Django REST Framework on the backend, and SQLite managed exclusively through the Django ORM. Styling uses Tailwind CSS with Framer Motion handling all animations. State is managed through Zustand with React Query for server synchronization.
The color palette is monochromatic and uses five values: #231208 as the darkest tone, #0D1A63 as the deep navy accent, #2845D6 as the primary bright blue, #EAEFEF as the light neutral, and #FAFAFA as the near-white surface. In dark mode, the main page background is #23120B. In light mode, the background uses #FAFAFA with #EAEFEF for cards and panels. The primary action color across both themes is #2845D6, and #0D1A63 serves as the secondary accent for badges, active states, and depth layering. The design is minimal and modern with a tech aesthetic — clean geometry, no decorative elements, subtle borders, and generous whitespace.
The application supports a dark and light theme toggle. The preference is stored in localStorage and applied synchronously via a data-theme attribute on the root HTML element before React mounts, preventing any flash of unstyled content. CSS custom properties drive all color values so that switching themes requires only changing the attribute on the root element.
The layout is fully responsive and must scale automatically across mobile, tablet, and desktop without any manual override by the user. On screens below 640px the layout is a single column with a bottom navigation bar. Between 640px and 1023px it shifts to a two-column grid with a collapsible side drawer. From 1024px and above it uses a three-column grid with a fixed sidebar. The maximum content width on large screens is 1400px centered in the viewport. All font sizes use rem units, with the base set to 16px on mobile and 18px on desktop. Typography scale runs from a 3rem display size for hero headings down to 0.875rem for captions and labels, with a line height of 1.6 for body text.
All animations must run at 60 frames per second and must only animate the transform and opacity CSS properties — never layout-triggering properties like height, width, margin, or padding. Page transitions use a 0.3 second ease-out fade combined with a 20px vertical slide. Button and hover micro-interactions stay between 0.15 and 0.2 seconds. Loading states use a shimmer gradient sweep animation at a 1.5 second loop rather than spinners. All non-essential animations must be disabled when the user has the prefers-reduced-motion system preference enabled.
Every page and module in the application must display skeleton loading components while data is being fetched. Skeleton loaders must mirror the exact shape, size, and layout of the real content they are replacing — cards show skeleton cards, tables show skeleton rows, form fields show skeleton input shapes, and headers show skeleton text blocks. The shimmer animation sweeps horizontally across the skeleton elements using a gradient from the base background color to a slightly lighter tone and back, looping at 1.5 seconds. Skeleton components must appear immediately on mount before any data request resolves, and they must transition into the real content using a smooth opacity fade once the data is ready. No spinner, loading text, or blank white area may appear anywhere in the application during a loading state.
For security, the application must block four specific attack vectors at every layer. Cross-site scripting is prevented by never using dangerouslySetInnerHTML in any React component, sanitizing all user-generated content through DOMPurify before it reaches the DOM, and enforcing a strict Content Security Policy header with no unsafe-inline or unsafe-eval directives. SQL injection is prevented by using Django ORM exclusively for all database operations — raw SQL string interpolation using f-strings or string concatenation is strictly forbidden throughout the entire codebase. Every API input must pass through a DRF Serializer with explicit field types, max_length constraints, and regex validators before any processing occurs. Race conditions are prevented by wrapping every endpoint that performs a read-modify-write operation in a Django atomic transaction using the @transaction.atomic decorator, applying select_for_update() on any field representing a balance, quota, or inventory count, and accepting an idempotency key header on all non-GET endpoints that stores the result and returns the cached response if the same key is seen again within 24 hours. Buffer overflow risks are mitigated by setting DATA_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE to 2.5 megabytes, FILE_UPLOAD_MAX_MEMORY_SIZE to 5 megabytes, and DATA_UPLOAD_MAX_NUMBER_FIELDS to 100 in Django settings, enforcing max_length on every user-facing model field, capping request body size at the reverse proxy level, and never returning unbounded querysets from any list endpoint — all lists must be paginated with a maximum page size of 20 items.
Django's built-in CSRF protection system must be fully enabled and kept in the middleware stack. The CsrfViewMiddleware must remain active in the MIDDLEWARE setting and must not be bypassed or exempted on any state-changing endpoint. The CSRF cookie must be set with CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE = True and CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True in production. On the frontend, every non-GET request sent by React must include the CSRF token read from the cookie and attached as the X-CSRFToken request header. Axios or the fetch wrapper must be configured globally to read the csrftoken cookie and inject it automatically on all POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE requests. No view, viewset, or API endpoint may use the @csrf_exempt decorator under any circumstances. CSRF protection works in addition to — not instead of — the JWT authentication layer, meaning every protected endpoint requires both a valid CSRF token and a valid access token before any logic is executed. This double validation provides defense in depth against both cross-site request forgery and unauthorized API access simultaneously.
Authentication uses a dual JWT token strategy. The access token expires in 15 minutes and the refresh token in 7 days. Both are stored exclusively in HttpOnly cookies with the Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict flags set. Tokens are never stored in localStorage or accessible to JavaScript. The login endpoint enforces a maximum of 5 attempts per 15-minute window per IP address, and all authentication events including failures are written to a dedicated security log.
Every action a user performs anywhere in the application must be recorded in a dedicated activity log module. Each log entry must capture the following fields without exception: the username and employee ID of the user who performed the action, the IP address of the client device resolved from the request headers with X-Forwarded-For support for requests behind a proxy, the MAC address of the client device collected from the network interface information available at the time of the request, the name of the module or page where the action was performed, a clear human-readable description of what the action was such as viewed payslip, submitted leave request, updated profile, deleted record, or logged in, the date and time the action was executed stored in UTC and displayed in the user's local timezone, and the HTTP method and endpoint path that was called. Logging must be implemented as a Django middleware class that intercepts every authenticated request and writes the log entry automatically after the response is generated, so that no individual view or endpoint needs to manually call a logging function. Log entries must be written to a dedicated ActivityLog database model and must never be editable or deletable by regular users or staff — only system administrators with a specific superuser flag may view or export logs. The log module must have its own dedicated page in the admin area displaying logs in a searchable, filterable, and paginated table where administrators can filter by username, module, action type, IP address, date range, and MAC address. Log writes must be non-blocking and must not slow down the response time of the action being logged — use Django's transaction.on_commit to write log entries after the response is committed. All log data must be retained indefinitely unless an administrator explicitly runs an archival or purge operation.
All cached data managed by React Query must be invalidated and refetched automatically whenever the system receives an update. This applies to every query cache across all features — no stale data should remain visible to the user after a data mutation or a server-side change is detected. On the backend, any write operation that modifies shared data must signal the affected cache keys for invalidation, and on the frontend, React Query's invalidateQueries must be called with the relevant query keys immediately after every successful mutation response.
In production, DEBUG must be False, SECRET_KEY must come from an environment variable, SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS must be set to 31536000, and SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT must be True. No secrets, API keys, or environment files may exist in version control at any point.

Input validation must be applied uniformly to every user-facing text input in the application, with the sole exception of password fields. All non-password inputs must reject the following characters on both keypress and paste: < > { } [ ] \ | ^ ~ ` " — = ' ' ^ * these characters have no legitimate use in free-text fields and are the primary vectors for XSS and template-injection attacks. Validation must be enforced on the frontend as the user types, showing an inline error message immediately below the offending field without blocking typing of valid characters. The same constraints must also be enforced at the serializer layer on the Django backend using a regex validator so that no invalid input can reach the database even if the frontend check is bypassed. Every input field must also enforce the max_length defined on its corresponding model field: the frontend maxLength attribute and any character-count display must match the backend model constraint exactly, and the save or submit action must remain disabled while any validation error is present. For event and holiday forms specifically, both the Create and Edit flows share identical validation rules — title (max 100 characters, no blocked characters) and note (max 300 characters, no blocked characters) — and the save button must be disabled until all fields pass validation. Destructive actions such as deleting a record must never execute on a single click; a confirmation step must appear — either an inline footer transformation or a dedicated confirmation modal — requiring the user to explicitly confirm before the deletion proceeds. During any save or delete operation the relevant action button must display the TextShimmer loading effect until the server responds.

Apply the text-shimmer effect to all action buttons—such as Submit, Create, Save Changes, and Delete—whenever the user clicks them and the system is in the process of submitting data to the database.

Component reuse is mandatory. Before creating any new UI component, always check the existing components in src/components/ui/ and src/components/ first. If a suitable component already exists — such as TextareaWithCharactersLeft for textareas with character counters, StatusPill for status badges, DataTable for paginated tables, or SearchBar for search inputs — it must be reused rather than reimplemented inline or duplicated. New components may only be introduced when explicitly instructed by the user or when no existing component can satisfy the requirement without modification. Every textarea field with a character limit must use TextareaWithCharactersLeft so that the character counter is displayed consistently across the entire application. All admin-remarks and free-text fields must enforce the same max_length on both the frontend (maxLength prop) and the backend serializer, and both must agree on the character limit.

Every admin page that requires role-based access control must implement the following standardized four-phase loading sequence without exception. Phase 1 — Spinner: the component mounts and immediately renders a centered spinning circle (h-8 w-8 animate-spin rounded-full border-2 border-[var(--color-border)] border-t-[#2845D6]) while the auth fetch has not yet begun or is in its very first tick. This is represented by authPhase === 'spinner'. Phase 2 — Checking permissions: after approximately 350 milliseconds, if the auth fetch has not yet resolved, the spinner transitions to a centered TextShimmer with the text "Checking permissions…" at text-sm with duration 1.4. This is represented by authPhase === 'checking'. If the fetch resolves before 350 ms, the component skips directly from spinner to done. Phase 3 — Skeleton: once the user is verified and authPhase becomes 'done', the full page layout renders immediately. Any components that are still loading data (charts, tables) display their skeleton variants — table rows show shimmer skeleton cells, charts show ChartSkeleton — so the page structure is visible while data resolves. Phase 4 — Full content: once all data has loaded, the real components replace the skeletons with a smooth opacity transition. This four-phase pattern must be implemented using a single state variable const [authPhase, setAuthPhase] = useState<'spinner' | 'checking' | 'done'>('spinner') and a setTimeout of 350 ms inside the auth useEffect that transitions to 'checking' if the fetch has not yet returned. The clearTimeout must be called as soon as the fetch resolves to prevent unnecessary phase transitions. All effects that depend on auth completion must check authPhase !== 'done' rather than a boolean flag.
